blocks.text: Assignment 03
Domain Model for University Timetable Optimization and Management System
Project: SE-Hakari-Bankai / UTOS

rows: Develop a domain model showing key concepts, attributes, and relationships.

blocks.text: Domain model focuses on real-world concepts, not software design.
1. Introduction

This document presents the domain model for the University Timetable Optimization and Management System (UTOS). It identifies the important real-world concepts in the project, their attributes, and the associations that connect them.

The model follows object-oriented analysis principles: concepts are domain things such as Teacher, CourseOffering, Room, Timeslot, Timetable, and ChangeRequest. Software details such as database tables, APIs, screens, local storage, and algorithms are intentionally excluded.
Figure 1: UTOS Domain Concepts Overview
2. Concept Identification

rows: Rooms and buildings are real facilities used for classes.
Course describes subject; offering connects it to term, teacher, and section.
These contain people, courses, and academic groups.
These preserve scheduling decisions and requested changes.
One scheduled meeting inside a timetable.
Rules that guide or restrict timetable creation.

blocks.text: 3. Key Concepts and Attributes
Attributes are kept simple. Relationships are not stored as foreign-key attributes in the domain model; they are shown as associations.

rows: name, policyType, priority, weight, isHardConstraint, isEnabled
name, status, qualityScore, hardConflictCount, softPenalty, unplacedSessionCount, createdAt
requestType, reason, urgency, preferredAlternative, coordinatorNote, administratorResponse, status, createdAt

blocks.text: 4. Domain Model Diagram
Figure 2 below shows the main UTOS concepts and their need-to-know relationships. Multiplicity appears in the relationship labels.
Figure 2: UTOS Domain Model Class Diagram
5. Associations and Multiplicity

rows: Availability is for a specific time
ClassSession is a line item of Timetable
Timetable is generated for a specific term

blocks.text: 6. Notes on Important Modeling Choices
Course and CourseOffering are separate because a course description can be reused across terms, sections, and teachers.
ClassSession is separate because it is a line item inside a timetable and must remember placement, lock status, and scheduling status.
Room belongs to Building because room proximity and facility management depend on building/location information.
TeacherAvailability is modeled separately because availability is a relationship between a teacher and a timeslot with its own attribute.
SchedulingPolicy is included because hard constraints and soft preferences are part of the timetable domain, not just code.
Database IDs and foreign keys are not attributes in this domain model; associations carry those relationships.
7. Short Explanation

The UTOS domain model represents a university scheduling environment. A university contains faculties and departments. Departments employ teachers, contain student sections, and offer courses. Students belong to sections, and sections take course offerings.

A course offering connects a course to a teacher, section, and academic term. Each offering is realized by one or more class sessions. A timetable contains class sessions, and each class session may be assigned a timeslot and a room. If a session has no room or timeslot, it is still part of the timetable but remains unplaced.

Scheduling policies, teacher availability, and holidays guide timetable validity and quality. Change requests allow teachers, students, coordinators, and administrators to communicate requested schedule changes. The model therefore captures the core concepts, attributes, and relationships needed for timetable generation, viewing, review, and improvement.
8. Excluded Software Artifacts

rows: Software storage design, not a real-world domain concept.
Foreign-key style attributes; represented by associations instead.

blocks.text: 9. Reviewed Project Diagrams

These existing project diagrams were reviewed while building the domain model. They are included as reference material only. The final domain model removes database, software architecture, and process details where they are not real-world domain concepts.

rows: Useful for noun extraction: calendar, holidays, timetable, teachers, rooms, courses, sections, timeslots, users, change requests.
Converted into domain concepts; PK/FK fields excluded.
Useful for use-case context: generation, review, publish, view, submit change request, approve/reject.
Used to confirm ChangeRequest, Timetable, SchedulingPolicy, and actor roles.
Useful for external actors: Admin, Teacher, Student, Department Coordinator, Facility Manager.
Used to validate role concepts and major associations.
Not part of core domain model because they describe software components or functions.

blocks.text: Reference Figure A: Existing ERD/data model used for concept extraction. Database keys and FK attributes are not carried into the final domain model.
Reference Figure B: Timetable generation workflow used to validate core actions and change request flow.
